Juan Dodyk

              Juan Dodyk
            
            
              
                
                    
                      
                        Email
                      
                    
                  
                                    
                    
                      
                        CV
                      
                    
                  
                                    
                    
                      
                        Google Scholar
                      
                    
                  
                                  
            
          
        
        
        
          
            I am an Assistant Professor in Political Science and
            Environmental Studies at Washington University in St. Louis.
            I received my Ph.D. in the Department of Government at
            Harvard University.
            I am a political economist interested in climate policy
            and business lobbying. I study how policymakers
            strategically design climate policies to overcome opposition
            from carbon-intensive industries, how interest groups use
            tactical coalitions to increase their effectiveness in
            informational lobbying, and how family firms build political
            power in developing countries. To address my research
            questions, I use formal models, original data collection,
            and causal inference methods.
            See my Job Market Paper, “Inefficient Policies in the
            Green Transition” here, my CV, and my research.
            
            
            
             Interests 
            Climate Policy and Politics
            Lobbying and Money in Politics
            Formal Political Theory
            
            
             Education 
            
            PhD in Government, 2025 
            Harvard University
            
            
            
            Bachelor’s degree in Mathematics, 2017 
            Universidad de Buenos Aires

articles.title: Research
articles.contents:
Published
Family Ties as Corporate Power (with Pablo Balán and Ignacio Puente). Journal of Politics, 2025. PDF. Online Appendix. Replication Archive. Media coverage: ProMarket.
The Political Behavior of Family Firms: Evidence from Brazil (with Pablo Balán and Ignacio Puente). World Development, 2022. PDF. Online Appendix. Replication Archive. Shiny App.
Working Papers
JOB MARKET PAPER: Inefficient Policies in the Green Transition.
International Coordination of Trade and Green Industrial Policies. Preliminary Draft.
An Informational Theory of Coalitional Lobbying. Draft. Slides.
A proof of Blackwell’s Theorem for separable metric state spaces. PDF.
Work in Progress
Policy Feedback Effects of Green Subsidies: Evidence from Firm Lobbying after the Inflation Reduction Act.
Dynamic Climate Policymaking under Majoritarian and Proportional Electoral Rules.
Endogenous Lobbying.
Outside Experts as Scapegoats: a Theory of Management Consultants.
A New Theory of Partisan Realignment.
Old stuff
Information, Policy Preferences and Voting. PDF.
Enchufes, Espejos y Tijeras: Efectos del Diseño de las Boletas Sobre el Comportamiento Electoral (with Juan Pablo Ruiz Nicolini). Revista SAAP, 2017. PDF.
Spatial Variation in Ecological Inference. PDF.

The book “Mathematics for Social Scientists” by Jonathan Kropko covers the topics we’ll see. You can read it here.
Gov 2006: Formal Theory II (Prof. Peter Buisseret)
I was a TA for this class twice – during Spring 2021 and Spring 2022. Slides I made for my classes:
Refinements of PBE.
The Principal-Agent Model.
Cheap Talk.
Global Games.
Electoral Competition.
Valence Advantage.
Repeated elections with two parties.
Accountability, Selection and Pandering.
Legislative Bargaining.
Lobbying.
Monotone comparative statics and economic policy.
Correction to Acemoglu-Robinson QJE (2000).
Real authority and expertise.
